channel.title: Terms on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Terms on emsenn.net

item.title: controlled vocabulary
item.description:
A controlled vocabulary is a fixed, curated set of terms used to classify content within a domain. Unlike a folksonomy, where users apply whatever labels they choose, a controlled vocabulary specifies in advance which terms are valid, how they relate to each other, and which term is preferred when synonyms exist. The purpose is precision: every classifier uses the same term for the same concept, which makes retrieval reliable.

The oldest and most influential controlled vocabularies come from library science. The Library of Congress Subject Headings (LCSH), published since 1909, provide a standardized set of terms for cataloging library materials. Medical Subject Headings (MeSH), maintained by the National Library of Medicine, organizes biomedical literature. Both systems use a consistent internal structure: preferred terms (the canonical label), non-preferred terms (synonyms that redirect to the canonical form), broader/narrower relationships (hierarchical links between general and specific concepts), and related terms (associative links between concepts that are connected but not hierarchically). This structure allows a searcher to find all content about a concept regardless of which synonym the original author might have used.

item.title: folksonomy
item.description:
A folksonomy is a classification system that emerges from the aggregate tagging behavior of users rather than from a centrally designed taxonomy. Thomas Vander Wal coined the term in 2004, combining “folk” and “taxonomy” to describe the collaborative categorization practices he observed on social bookmarking sites like del.icio.us. Unlike a controlled vocabulary maintained by domain experts, a folksonomy grows organically as users apply whatever labels make sense to them.

Vander Wal identified two varieties. A broad folksonomy arises when many independent users tag the same object — the aggregate reveals which tags are most popular for each item, producing a rough consensus through statistical weight. del.icio.us bookmarks were the canonical example: hundreds of users bookmarking the same URL would independently converge on similar tags, and the distribution of tags across users revealed which labels the community found most useful. A narrow folksonomy arises when one person (or a few people) tags their own content. Flickr photo tags were the canonical example: the photographer labeled their own images, making unsearchable visual content findable through text. A personal knowledge vault is a narrow folksonomy of one — the narrowest possible case.

item.title: hashtag
item.description: A hashtag is a word or phrase prefixed with the # symbol, used as a tag within social media and web platforms to mark content as relevant to a topic. The convention was proposed by Chris Messina on Twitter in August 2007 as a way to group conversations around shared topics without requiring any platform infrastructure beyond text search. Twitter initially had no formal tag system; Messina’s proposal succeeded because it was simple, required no platform support, and could be adopted unilaterally by any user.

item.title: keyword
item.description: A keyword is a term or short phrase assigned to a piece of content to describe its subject matter for the purpose of indexing and discovery. Keywords serve a similar function to tags but carry a connotation of curation and restraint: where tags in a folksonomy are applied freely and in unlimited quantity, keywords in academic and publishing contexts are selected deliberately, limited in number (typically three to five, rarely more than ten), and expected to precisely characterize the work’s contribution to its field.

item.title: tag wrangling
item.description:
Tag wrangling is a curation practice in which volunteer editors organize a folksonomy by designating canonical tags, linking synonyms, and creating grouping relationships — without restricting what users can tag at the point of creation. The practice was developed and named by Archive of Our Own (AO3), a fanfiction archive launched in 2009, and represents one of the most successful real-world syntheses of free tagging and controlled vocabulary.

AO3 uses six distinct tag types (ratings, archive warnings, categories, fandoms, relationships, characters, and freeform/additional tags). Users can enter any freeform text as a tag. Tag wranglers — a distributed team of volunteers — then organize the resulting namespace through three operations. First, they designate canonical tags as the preferred form for each concept: “Sherlock Holmes/John Watson” becomes the canonical form that “Johnlock” and “Holmes/Watson” redirect to. Second, they link synonyms to their canonical form so that searching either term finds content tagged with any variant. Third, they create metatags that group related-but-not-synonymous concepts — a metatag for “Coffee Shop AUs” might gather freeform tags like “barista AU” and “coffeehouse romance” under a common umbrella.
